blurb: Nelson Mandela's favourite dish — samp and sugar beans slow-cooked into a creamy, comforting porridge.

prov.popular_in: Xhosa homes and across South Africa
prov.famous_for: Being Nelson Mandela's favourite dish and for its nourishing, filling qualities
prov.consists_of: Samp (crushed maize kernels) and sugar beans soaked, then slow-cooked with onions, butter and stock until creamy
prov.name_origin: Xhosa traditional dish; 'umngqusho' refers to the cracked corn and bean mixture
prov.why_loved: Humble, nourishing and deeply comforting — the taste of Xhosa home cooking
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — samp and beans soaked overnight, simmered with onions and stock 2–3 hours until soft and creamy, finished with butter

brief.lead: Umngqusho is Xhosa comfort food at its finest — samp (crushed maize) and sugar beans transformed through slow cooking into a rich, creamy porridge that sustained Nelson Mandela through his life. This humble dish embodies the Xhosa philosophy of ubuntu, filling the belly and warming the soul.
expect: Ready in 3 hours plus overnight soaking.
A thick, creamy porridge with tender beans and subtle sweetness.

tl: Rinse samp and beans, cover with cold water and soak overnight
Drain soaking liquid, transfer to pot with stock and onions
Add potatoes if using, check liquid level
Stir in butter and season until creamy
steps: Rinse and soak
Rinse samp and beans thoroughly under cold water until water runs clear. Place in a large bowl, cover with plenty of cold water and soak overnight (8–12 hours) to soften.
Soaking hydrates the hard starch and reduces cooking time dramatically.
Drain and simmer
Drain soaking liquid and transfer to a large pot. Add stock, diced onion and salt. Bring to the boil, then reduce heat and simmer gently, covered, for 1½–2 hours.
Stock provides depth; gentle simmering prevents bursting and ensures even cooking.
Check and stir
Check liquid level every 30 minutes — the mixture should be soupy, not dry. Add hot water or stock if needed. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking.
Samp absorbs liquid rapidly; keeping it moist prevents scorching.
Add potatoes (optional)
If using potatoes, add them now. Simmer another 30–45 minutes until samp and beans are completely tender and potatoes are cooked through.
Potatoes add bulk and sweetness, making the dish even more filling.
Finish with butter
Remove from heat. Stir in butter until melted and incorporated. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt and pepper. The texture should be thick and creamy, like a porridge.
Butter enriches and binds; creaminess comes from starch release.
Rest and serve
Let stand 5 minutes to thicken slightly. Serve warm as a side to stews, grilled meats or enjoy on its own.
Resting allows starches to set for the perfect consistency.
pair: The bold fruit and gentle tannins complement the earthy, creamy texture.
The gentle honeyed notes echo the natural sweetness of the beans.

blurb: Durban's celebratory feast — layered rice and meat in aromatic spices, the crown of Durban Indian cuisine.

prov.popular_in: Durban Indian homes and special occasions across South Africa
prov.famous_for: The complex layering of spiced meat, rice and potatoes that creates distinct, harmonious flavours
prov.consists_of: Meat marinated in yoghurt and spices, layered with par-cooked rice, potatoes and whole spices, then steamed until fragrant
prov.name_origin: From 'biryani', adapted by Durban's Indian community into a distinct South African version
prov.why_loved: Rich, aromatic and festive — the centrepiece of any South African Indian celebration
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — meat marinated 2–4 hours, par-cooked, layered with rice and whole spices, covered and steamed on low heat 45–60 min until rice is fluffy and fragrant

brief.lead: Breyani is Durban Indian cuisine at its most lavish — marinated meat, fragrant rice and potatoes layered with whole spices and steamed into a single pot of aromatic perfection. This festive dish brings people together, the anticipation of lifting the lid part of the ritual.
expect: Ready in 2 hours plus 2–4 hours marinating.
Tender meat, fluffy rice and melting potatoes in a rich spiced aroma.

ing: boneless chicken thighs or lamb, cubed
biryani masala or mild curry powder
tl: Marinate meat in yoghurt and spices
Fry onions, brown meat, add tomatoes and simmer
Par-boil rice with whole spices until 70% done
Layer meat, rice, potatoes and butter
Cover tightly and steam on very low heat
Rest 10 minutes, fluff and serve with atchar
steps: Marinate the meat
Mix meat with yoghurt, ginger-garlic paste, biryani masala, turmeric, salt, lemon juice and half the oil. Cover and refrigerate for 2–4 hours.
Yoghurt tenderises and carries spices deep into the meat.
Fry aromatics
Heat remaining oil in a heavy pot. Fry sliced onions until golden brown. Remove half for topping. Add marinated meat (reserve excess marinade) and brown on all sides.
Caramelised onions provide depth; browning develops flavour.
Simmer meat
Add tomatoes and reserved marinade. Simmer covered for 15–20 minutes until meat is half-cooked. Add potatoes and cook 5 minutes more.
Building a base sauce creates moisture for steaming rice.
Par-cook rice
Meanwhile, bring 1.5 litres water to boil with cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, bay leaf and 1 tsp salt. Add rice and boil until 70% cooked (grain still has a firm centre). Drain well.
Par-cooking ensures rice finishes steaming without going mushy.
Layer and seal
Transfer meat mixture to the pot. Spread rice evenly over meat. Top with fried onions and dot with butter. Cover tightly with foil then lid.
Layering protects rice; butter enriches and bastes.
Steam
Place on very low heat for 45–60 minutes. Or bake at 180°C for the same time. The trapped steam should cook rice completely.
Gentle steaming fuses flavours; tight seal is critical.
Rest and serve
Remove from heat and let rest 10 minutes (do not open lid). Gently fluff with a fork, mixing layers slightly. Garnish with coriander and serve with atchar.
Resting sets starch; gentle folding keeps layers intact.
pair: The gentle sweetness balances the spice without overpowering.
Classic pairing for spiced dishes; sweetness cools the heat.
Cold and sweet, it cuts through the rich aromatics.

blurb: Cape Malay skewers — lamb and dried apricots marinated in a tangy curry-spiced sauce, grilled to sticky perfection.

prov.famous_for: The sweet-savoury contrast of lamb and dried apricot on a single skewer, glazed with a spicy apricot marinade
prov.consists_of: Lamb cubes and dried apricots threaded onto skewers, marinated in apricot jam, curry powder, vinegar and spices, then grilled
prov.name_origin: From 'satay' via Cape Malay adaptation; 'sosatie' combines 'saus' (sauce) and 'satay'
prov.why_loved: The combination of tender meat, sweet fruit and spicy glaze — Cape Malay flavour on a stick
prov.method_detail: Fire/oven — meat and apricots marinated 4–24 hours, threaded onto skewers, grilled over coals or at 180°C 20–25 min, basted frequently

brief.lead: Sosaties are Cape Malay's brilliant answer to satay — plump lamb cubes and sweet dried apricots marinated in a tangy curry-spiced apricot sauce, then grilled to sticky perfection. The interplay of tender meat, chewy fruit and spicy-sweet glaze makes them the ultimate braai starter.
expect: Ready in 2 hours plus 4–24 hours marinating.
Tender lamb with sweet-spicy glazed apricots on skewers.

tl: Combine marinade ingredients and coat lamb
Thread lamb, apricots and onion onto skewers
Grill over coals or at 180°C, basting every 5 minutes
Rest 5 minutes before serving with yellow rice
steps: Make marinade
Mix apricot jam, vinegar, oil, curry powder, brown sugar, ginger-garlic paste, grated onion, turmeric, salt and pepper. Whisk until smooth.
Balancing sweet, sour and spicy creates the characteristic Cape Malay flavour.
Marinate lamb
Place lamb cubes in a dish. Pour marinade over, turning to coat thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for 4–24 hours — the longer, the better.
Marinating tenderises and infuses flavour deep into the meat.
Thread skewers
Thread ingredients onto skewers in alternating pattern: lamb cube, apricot, onion wedge. Finish with a lamb cube. If wrapping, stretch bacon around each lamb cube and thread through.
Alternating ingredients ensures even cooking and flavour distribution.
Prepare grill
Heat braai coals to medium-hot (you can hold your hand above for 3–4 seconds) or preheat oven to 180°C. Thread remaining onion wedges onto separate skewers.
Medium heat allows cooking through without burning the glaze.
Grill sosaties
Grill skewers for 20–25 minutes, turning frequently and basting with reserved marinade every 5 minutes. The glaze should caramelise and darken sticky-golden.
Frequent basting builds layers of flavour; turning prevents burning.
Check doneness
Lamb should be cooked through (pink is safe for young lamb, but well-done is traditional) and apricots plump and glossy. If in oven, grill for the last 3 minutes for char.
Caramelisation gives the signature sticky glaze.
Rest and serve
Rest 5 minutes loosely covered. Serve hot with yellow rice, atchar and a crisp salad.
Resting redistributes juices; traditional sides complete the meal.
pair: The sweet fruit notes echo the apricots while acidity balances the spice.
South Africa · Castle or Black Label
Cold and crisp — a classic braai pairing that cuts through the sweet glaze.
The lychee and rose notes complement the curry spices beautifully.

blurb: Cape coastal tradition — whole snoek butterflied and grilled over coals with a sticky apricot jam and garlic glaze.

prov.popular_in: Cape coastal braais, especially in the Western Cape
prov.famous_for: The sweet-salty apricot glaze that caramelises on the smoky, oily snoek skin
prov.consists_of: Whole snoek butterflied, brushed with a mixture of butter, garlic, lemon juice and apricot jam, grilled over hot coals
prov.name_origin: 'Snoek' is the Dutch name for the snake mackerel abundant in Cape waters; the apricot glaze is a Cape tradition
prov.why_loved: Smoky, sweet, salty and succulent — the taste of a Cape summer evening by the sea
prov.method_detail: Fire — snoek butterflied, brushed with apricot-garlic glaze, grilled over coals 8–12 min per side, basted frequently until glazed and cooked

brief.lead: Snoek braai with apricot jam is pure Cape coastal magic — oily, sweet-salty snoek grilled over coals until the skin chars and the flesh flakes, all glazed with a sticky mixture of apricot jam, butter, garlic and lemon. It's the taste of summer evenings by the sea.
expect: Flaky, smoky fish with a sweet-spicy caramelised glaze.

tl: Butterfly snoek, make glaze, heat coals
Brush fish with lemon, season with salt and pepper
Place on braai grid, grill 8–10 minutes per side
Brush with apricot glaze, grill 2–3 minutes each side
Rest briefly, garnish and serve with potato salad
steps: Butterfly snoek
If whole, ask your fishmonger to butterfly it or do it yourself: cut along both sides of the backbone, remove bone, open fish flat like a book. Rinse and pat dry.
Butterflying ensures even cooking and maximum surface area for glazing.
Make glaze
Mix apricot jam, melted butter, crushed garlic, lemon juice and sweet chilli sauce (if using). Whisk until smooth and emulsified.
Butter carries the apricot flavour and creates a caramelising glaze.
Season fish
Brush snoek with lemon juice. Season generously with salt and pepper on both sides — the oiliness needs salt to balance.
Salt balances the rich oil; lemon adds brightness.
Heat braai
Prepare a medium-hot fire (coals white-hot but not flaming). Place a braai grid over the coals. Alternatively, preheat oven to 200°C.
Medium heat cooks through without burning the delicate flesh.
Grill first side
Place snoek skin-side down on the grid. Grill for 8–10 minutes until skin chars lightly and flesh begins to opaque. Do not turn yet.
Skin-side down protects flesh and renders fat for crispy skin.
Turn and glaze
Carefully turn. Brush liberally with apricot glaze. Grill 8–10 minutes more until fish flakes easily and glaze caramelises sticky-golden.
Glazing at the end prevents burning while achieving caramelisation.
Finish and serve
Remove from heat. Let rest 2–3 minutes loosely covered. Sprinkle with coriander and serve with lemon wedges, potato salad and fresh bread.
Resting keeps juices in; coriander adds fresh contrast.
pair: The high acidity and herbaceous notes cut through the rich oily fish.
A cold beer balances the sweet glaze and smoky oil perfectly.
The gentle sweetness complements the apricot glaze without competing.

blurb: Cape Malay's Easter classic — fried white fish steeped in a sweet-sour curry-spiced vinegar with onions.

prov.popular_in: Cape Malay homes and especially popular at Easter
prov.famous_for: The complex sweet-sour pickle with curry spices that preserves and flavours the fish
prov.consists_of: White fish dusted in flour and turmeric, fried, then steeped in a vinegar-curry pickle with onions, whole spices and sugar
prov.name_origin: Dutch preservation techniques adapted with Cape Malay spices; traditionally made to preserve fish over Easter
prov.why_loved: The perfect balance of sweet, sour and spicy — better with each day it pickles
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — fish dusted in turmeric flour and fried, pickling liquid of vinegar, sugar, onions, curry powder and whole spices simmered and poured over, cooled, refrigerated 24 hours

brief.lead: Pickled fish is Cape Malay's brilliant preservation dish turned delicacy — white fish fried until golden, then steeped in a complex pickle of vinegar, onions, curry spices and sugar. Traditionally made for Easter when no meat was eaten, it's now beloved for its sweet-sour depth.
expect: Ready in 45 minutes plus 24 hours pickling.
Tender fish in a tangy, aromatic curry pickle — better each day.

ing: white fish fillets (hake, kingklip or yellowtail)
tl: Fry fish, simmer pickle liquid, assemble and refrigerate
Coat fish in turmeric flour, fry until golden
Simmer onions and spices in vinegar, pour over fish
Let cool completely, then refrigerate 24 hours
Bring to room temperature, garnish and serve with bread
steps: Coat fish
Mix flour, turmeric and salt. Cut fish into serving-sized pieces. Dust fish lightly in the seasoned flour, shaking off excess.
Turmeric gives colour and subtle earthiness; flour creates a light crust.
Fry fish
Heat 5mm oil in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry fish 2–3 minutes per side until golden brown and just cooked through. Drain on paper.
Frying seals in moisture and provides structure for pickling.
Start pickle
In a separate pan, fry sliced onions in a little oil until softened (5 minutes). Add curry powder and stir for 1 minute until fragrant.
Blooming curry powder releases its aromatic oils.
Add vinegar
Add vinegar, brown sugar, whole spices (allspice, cloves, coriander, bay leaves, cinnamon) and salt. Simmer for 5 minutes until sugar dissolves.
Whole spices provide gentle background notes; vinegar preserves and cuts richness.
Assemble
Layer fried fish and onion-spice mixture in a glass dish, ending with onions on top. Pour over hot pickling liquid to cover completely.
Hot liquid begins pickling immediately; layers ensure even coverage.
Cool and pickle
Let cool completely at room temperature. Cover tightly and refrigerate for at least 24 hours, ideally 2–3 days for flavours to develop.
Cooling prevents condensation; time develops complexity.
Serve
Bring to room temperature 30 minutes before serving. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve with lemon wedges and crusty bread.
Room temperature releases fullest flavours; bread soaks up the pickle.
pair: The high acidity matches the vinegar while stone fruit notes complement the spice.
The tannic backbone and gentle sweetness mirror the pickle's complexity.
Cutting acidity balances the sweet-sour pickle perfectly.
